# React UI Component Libraries & Design Systems

## Enterprise-Grade Options

**Ant Design React**
- Description: "An enterprise-class UI design language and React-based implementation"
- Features: Comprehensive component set, Material Design-inspired, widely adopted by major organizations
- GitHub: High star count (major open source project)

**Material UI (MUI)**
- Description: Components implementing Google's Material Design specification
- Features: Extensive customization, TypeScript support, example projects available
- Use Case: Enterprise applications requiring Material Design patterns

**Chakra UI**
- Description: "Modular & Accessible UI Components for your React Applications"
- Features: Accessibility-focused, themeable, composable architecture
- Strength: Developer experience and accessibility compliance

**Polaris React (Shopify)**
- Description: Component library based on Shopify's Polaris design system
- Features: Production-tested, e-commerce-optimized, design system alignment
- Use Case: Retail and marketplace applications

**Carbon (IBM)**
- Description: React components following IBM's Carbon design system
- Features: Enterprise-grade, accessibility standards, extensive documentation
- Adoption: Used across IBM product portfolio

**Blueprint (Palantir)**
- Description: "A React-based UI toolkit for building complex, data-dense web interfaces for desktop applications"
- Features: Data visualization support, professional applications focus
- Strength: Complex application requirements

**Base UI (Uber)**
- Description: React component library implementing Base UI design
- Features: Flexible, unstyled-first approach, modern architecture
- Use Case: Organizations needing design flexibility

**Elastic (EUI)**
- Description: "Collection of React UI components for quickly building user interfaces at Elastic"
- Features: Enterprise dashboard development, dark mode support
- Production: Used in Elastic products

**Semantic UI React**
- Description: "Official React integration for Semantic UI"
- Adoption: Used by Netflix, Amazon, and major organizations
- Strength: Intuitive component API, extensive examples

**Evergreen (Segment)**
- Description: React UI Framework designed for Segment's web products
- Features: Accessibility compliance, customizable themes, polished components
- Use Case: Web application development

**PatternFly React**
- Description: "Set of React components for the PatternFly project built on Bootstrap 3"
- Features: Enterprise UI patterns, responsive design
- Adoption: Red Hat and enterprise clients

**Instructure UI**
- Description: "UI Component Library made by Instructure, Inc"
- Focus: Educational technology applications
- Production: Developed for real-world ed-tech platforms

**Lightning Design System (Salesforce)**
- Description: "React implementation of the Salesforce Lightning Design System"
- Features: Enterprise CRM-optimized, official Salesforce implementation
- Adoption: Salesforce ecosystem

**RSuite (React Suite)**
- Description: "Set of react component libraries for enterprise system products"
- Features: Localization support, enterprise-focused components
- Use Case: Business applications

These libraries balance production readiness, accessibility, customization, and community support for serious application development.

# MMS Demo Implementation TODO

Last updated: 2025-12-09 (02:10 UTC)

---

## QUICK CONTEXT (Read First When You Wake Up)

**What is this project:** Mileage Management System for Keolis MHI (Dubai Metro)
**Value:** $147,500 | **Deadline:** Demo this week | **Current phase:** Phase 0 (Demo Recovery)

**Stack decided:**

- Frontend: React Router v7 + Cloudscape
- Backend: Hono + Bun
- Solver: Python + HiGHS MIP
- **Database: MongoDB** (decision confirmed - use SM document-client code)

**What already works:**

- Solver Python: 16 tests passing
- API: 14 tests passing
- Frontend: What-If Simulator connected to real solver
- Charts: Workload visualization

---

## üö® SESSION STATE - DECEMBER 9, 2025 (02:10 UTC)

### What Was Being Done

Recreando o app MMS com o template oficial do React Router v7 porque o setup anterior tinha problemas de SSR.

### Current File System State

```
apps/
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ mms/           # NEW - Created from official template (npx create-react-router@latest)
‚îÇ                  # Template copied, dependencies were installing when interrupted
‚îÇ                  # Uses npm by default - NEEDS to be converted to pnpm
‚îÇ
‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ mms-old/       # OLD - Previous setup with SSR issues
    ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ app/
        ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ routes/
        ‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ dashboard.tsx
        ‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ fleet.tsx
        ‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ schedule.tsx
        ‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ what-if.tsx
        ‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ calendar.tsx      # NEW - Calendar view implemented
        ‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ _layout.tsx
        ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ lib/
            ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ demo-data.ts      # UPDATED - 79 ALT + 49 KNK realistic data
            ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ export-utils.ts   # NEW - Excel/CSV export utilities
            ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ api.ts
```

### Files Created This Session (in mms-old, need to migrate)

1. **`apps/mms-old/app/routes/calendar.tsx`** - Calendar view with:
   - Weekly schedule visualization
   - Color-coded events by maintenance type (A/B/C/D checks)
   - Capacity utilization progress bars
   - Click for event details modal
   - Export dropdown (Excel, PDF, Maximo)
   - Filter by maintenance type

2. **`apps/mms-old/app/lib/export-utils.ts`** - Export utilities:
   - `generateExcelExport()` - 6-tab workbook (Executive Summary, Full Schedule, Weekly Workload, Train Summary, Compliance Report, Cost Projection)
   - `generateMaximoCSV()` - IBM Maximo import format
   - `downloadExcel()`, `downloadCSV()` - Browser download helpers

3. **`apps/mms-old/app/lib/demo-data.ts`** - Updated with realistic fleet:
   - 79 Alstom trains (ALT-001 to ALT-079)
   - 49 Kinki Sharyo trains (KNK-001 to KNK-049)
   - Staggered mileage to avoid maintenance clustering
   - Dubai-specific depots (Al Rashidiya, Jebel Ali)

### Dependencies Added (in mms-old)

```json
{
  "xlsx": "0.18.5"  // For Excel export
}
```

### Git Status Before Session End

```
Changes not staged for commit:
  modified:   apps/mms/app/routes/calendar.tsx
  modified:   apps/mms/package.json
  modified:   pnpm-lock.yaml

Untracked files:
  apps/mms/.react-router/
  apps/mms/app/lib/export-utils.ts
```

**Note:** These changes are now in `apps/mms-old/`

### Commits Made This Session

1. `885ed0e` - docs(demo): add comprehensive demo strategy, implementation plan, and script
2. `d99c369` - feat(demo): realistic Dubai Metro fleet data
3. `13e409c` - feat(demo): add maintenance calendar view

---

## NEXT SESSION - IMMEDIATE ACTIONS

### Priority 1: Fix MMS App Setup

The React Router v7 app had SSR issues. A new app was created from the official template but was interrupted during dependency installation.

**Steps:**

1. **Check new mms app state:**
   ```bash
   cd /Users/anotonio.silva/antonio/uptime/apps/mms
   ls -la
   cat package.json
   ```

2. **If incomplete, recreate properly:**
   ```bash
   cd /Users/anotonio.silva/antonio/uptime/apps
   rm -rf mms
   # Use pnpm instead of npm
   pnpm create react-router@latest mms
   ```

3. **Migrate code from mms-old:**
   - Copy routes: `dashboard.tsx`, `fleet.tsx`, `schedule.tsx`, `what-if.tsx`, `calendar.tsx`, `_layout.tsx`
   - Copy lib: `demo-data.ts`, `export-utils.ts`, `api.ts`
   - Update package.json: add Cloudscape, xlsx, workspace dependencies

4. **Configure for monorepo:**
   - Update package.json name to `@uptime/mms`
   - Add workspace dependencies: `@uptime/types`, `@uptime/i18n`
   - Add API proxy in vite.config.ts

5. **Test:**
   ```bash
   cd /Users/anotonio.silva/antonio/uptime
   pnpm install
   pnpm dev
   ```

### Priority 2: Complete Excel Export

The export functionality is implemented but wasn't tested because dev server wasn't running.

- Test Excel download works
- Verify 6 tabs are created correctly
- Test Maximo CSV format

### Priority 3: Commit Changes

After migration is complete and tested:

```bash
git add .
git commit -m "feat(mms): migrate to official React Router v7 template with calendar and exports"
```

---

## Technical Context

### Why SSR Setup Failed

The original MMS app was missing `entry.server.tsx`. React Router v7 requires this file for SSR mode. The error was:

```
Error: Could not determine server runtime. Please install @react-router/node,
or provide a custom entry.server.tsx/jsx file in your app directory.
```

**Solution:** Use official template which includes proper SSR setup, or run `npx react-router reveal` to generate entry files.

### React Router v7 Latest Version

As of December 2025:
- `react-router`: 7.10.1
- `@react-router/node`: 7.10.1
- `@react-router/dev`: 7.10.1
- `@react-router/serve`: 7.10.1

### Key Documentation Links

- [React Router Installation](https://reactrouter.com/start/framework/installation)
- [entry.server.tsx](https://reactrouter.com/api/framework-conventions/entry.server.tsx)
- [SSR Configuration](https://reactrouter.com/start/framework/rendering)

---

| **Realistic Fleet Data** | ‚úÖ Done | `apps/mms-old/app/lib/demo-data.ts` | 79 ALT + 49 KNK |
| **Calendar View** | ‚úÖ Done | `apps/mms-old/app/routes/calendar.tsx` | Needs testing |
| **Excel Export** | ‚úÖ Done | `apps/mms-old/app/lib/export-utils.ts` | Needs testing |
| **Dashboard** | ‚öÝÔ∏è Partial | `apps/mms-old/app/routes/dashboard.tsx` | Needs real data |

| **Schedule Page** | ‚öÝÔ∏è Partial | `apps/mms-old/app/routes/schedule.tsx` | Needs real data |
| **PDF Report** | ‚ùå Not Started | - | Use jsPDF |
| **ROI Calculator** | ‚ùå Not Started | - | Static display |
| **Maximo Export** | ‚öÝÔ∏è Partial | `apps/mms-old/app/lib/export-utils.ts` | CSV format ready |

---

## Strategic Documents

These documents were created this session and contain critical demo strategy:

1. **`docs/internal/DEMO-STRATEGY.md`** - Complete strategy with:
   - ROI evidence (Deutsche Bahn EUR 74M, McKinsey 15-25%)
   - Psychology/marketing techniques
   - Answer to "Have you done this before?"
   - Validation system design
   - Feature matrix

2. **`docs/internal/DEMO-IMPLEMENTATION-PLAN.md`** - 5-day schedule with:
   - Priority tiers (P0/P1/P2)
   - Technical specifications
   - Fallback plans

3. **`docs/internal/DEMO-SCRIPT.md`** - Presentation script with:
   - 5 acts structure
   - Q&A preparation
   - Follow-up email template

---

## Important Reminders

1. **Always use pnpm**, not npm
2. **Always search latest docs** when stuck - we're in December 2025
3. **Commit progress** after completing each logical unit
4. **Test before committing** - run `pnpm dev` to verify
5. **Check versions** - use `pnpm view <package> version` not npm

---

## Reference

# Terminal 1: API
cd packages/api && bun run dev

# Terminal 2: Frontend (after migration)
cd apps/mms && pnpm dev

cd packages/api && bun test                                           # 14 tests

1‚Üí---
     2‚Üítitle: Technology Stack Recommendations
     3‚Üícategory: implementation
     4‚Üídifficulty: intermediate
     5‚Üítags: [tech-stack, typescript, python, postgresql, architecture]
     6‚Üírelated_docs: [02-data-model, 03-api-design]
     7‚Üísummary: Recommended technology stack for fleet maintenance scheduling systems with rationale for each choice.
     8‚Üíversion: 1.0
     9‚Üílast_updated: 2025-12-07
    10‚Üí---
    11‚Üí
    12‚Üí# Technology Stack Recommendations
    13‚Üí
    14‚ÜíThis document provides technology recommendations for building a fleet maintenance scheduling system, with rationale for each choice.
    15‚Üí
    16‚Üí## Stack Overview
    17‚Üí
    18‚Üí```typescript
    19‚Üíinterface RecommendedStack {
    20‚Üí  backend: {
    21‚Üí    primary: 'TypeScript + Node.js (Bun runtime)';
    22‚Üí    solver: 'Python (HiGHS + OR-Tools)';
    23‚Üí    api: 'Hono framework';
    24‚Üí  };
    25‚Üí
    26‚Üí  database: {
    27‚Üí    primary: 'PostgreSQL 16+';
    28‚Üí    timeSeries: 'TimescaleDB extension';
    29‚Üí    cache: 'Redis';
    30‚Üí  };
    31‚Üí
    32‚Üí  frontend: {
    33‚Üí    framework: 'React 18+ with TypeScript';
    34‚Üí    meta: 'Next.js or Astro';
    35‚Üí    visualization: 'ECharts';
    36‚Üí  };
    37‚Üí
    38‚Üí  infrastructure: {
    39‚Üí    containers: 'Docker';
    40‚Üí    orchestration: 'Docker Compose or Kubernetes';
    41‚Üí    cloud: 'AWS/GCP/Azure agnostic';
    42‚Üí  };
    43‚Üí}
    44‚Üí```
    45‚Üí
    46‚Üí## Backend: TypeScript + Bun
    47‚Üí
    48‚Üí### Why TypeScript
    49‚Üí
    50‚Üí```typescript
    51‚Üíinterface TypeScriptBenefits {
    52‚Üí  typeSafety: 'Catch errors at compile time';
    53‚Üí  developer: 'Excellent IDE support, refactoring';
    54‚Üí  ecosystem: 'Largest package ecosystem (npm)';
    55‚Üí  hiring: 'Large developer pool';
    56‚Üí  fullStack: 'Same language frontend and backend';
    57‚Üí}
    58‚Üí```
    59‚Üí
    60‚Üí### Why Bun Runtime
    61‚Üí
    62‚Üí```typescript
    63‚Üíinterface BunBenefits {
    64‚Üí  performance: '3-4x faster than Node.js';
    65‚Üí  native: 'TypeScript without transpilation';
    66‚Üí  bundler: 'Built-in bundler and test runner';
    67‚Üí  compatibility: 'Node.js API compatible';
    68‚Üí  modern: 'ESM-first, Web APIs built-in';
    69‚Üí}
    70‚Üí```
    71‚Üí
    72‚Üí### Setup Example
    73‚Üí
    74‚Üí```bash
    75‚Üí# Initialize Bun project
    76‚Üíbun init
    77‚Üí
    78‚Üí# Install dependencies
    79‚Üíbun add hono drizzle-orm postgres zod
    80‚Üíbun add -d typescript @types/node
    81‚Üí```
    82‚Üí
    83‚Üí```typescript
    84‚Üí// src/index.ts
    85‚Üíimport { Hono } from 'hono';
    86‚Üíimport { cors } from 'hono/cors';
    87‚Üíimport { logger } from 'hono/logger';
    88‚Üí
    89‚Üíconst app = new Hono();
    90‚Üí
    91‚Üíapp.use('*', logger());
    92‚Üíapp.use('*', cors());
    93‚Üí
    94‚Üíapp.get('/health', (c) => c.json({ status: 'ok' }));
    95‚Üí
    96‚Üíexport default {
    97‚Üí  port: process.env.PORT ?? 3000,
    98‚Üí  fetch: app.fetch
    99‚Üí};
   100‚Üí```
   101‚Üí
   102‚Üí## Solver: Python
   103‚Üí
   104‚Üí### Why Python for Solver
   105‚Üí
   106‚Üí```typescript
   107‚Üíinterface PythonSolverBenefits {
   108‚Üí  libraries: 'Best optimization library support';
   109‚Üí  highs: 'Native highspy bindings';
   110‚Üí  ortools: 'Official Google OR-Tools support';
   111‚Üí  scientific: 'NumPy, Pandas for data processing';
   112‚Üí  academic: 'Optimization research uses Python';
   113‚Üí}
   114‚Üí```
   115‚Üí
   116‚Üí### Integration Pattern
   117‚Üí
   118‚Üí```typescript
   119‚Üí// TypeScript calls Python solver via subprocess
   120‚Üíimport { spawn } from 'bun';
   121‚Üí
   122‚Üíinterface SolverInput {
   123‚Üí  events: MaintenanceEvent[];
   124‚Üí  periods: number[];
   125‚Üí  capacity: number;
   126‚Üí  timeLimit: number;
   127‚Üí}
   128‚Üí
   129‚Üíinterface SolverOutput {
   130‚Üí  status: string;
   131‚Üí  schedule: Record<string, number>;
   132‚Üí  objective: number;
   133‚Üí  solveTime: number;
   134‚Üí}
   135‚Üí
   136‚Üíasync function callSolver(input: SolverInput): Promise<SolverOutput> {
   137‚Üí  const proc = spawn(['python3', 'solver/main.py'], {
   138‚Üí    stdin: 'pipe',
   139‚Üí    stdout: 'pipe',
   140‚Üí    stderr: 'pipe'
   141‚Üí  });
   142‚Üí
   143‚Üí  // Send input
   144‚Üí  proc.stdin.write(JSON.stringify(input));
   145‚Üí  proc.stdin.end();
   146‚Üí
   147‚Üí  // Read output
   148‚Üí  const stdout = await new Response(proc.stdout).text();
   149‚Üí  const stderr = await new Response(proc.stderr).text();
   150‚Üí
   151‚Üí  const exitCode = await proc.exited;
   152‚Üí  if (exitCode !== 0) {
   153‚Üí    throw new Error(`Solver failed: ${stderr}`);
   154‚Üí  }
   155‚Üí
   156‚Üí  return JSON.parse(stdout);
   157‚Üí}
   158‚Üí```
   159‚Üí
   160‚Üí```python
   161‚Üí# solver/main.py
   162‚Üíimport sys
   163‚Üíimport json
   164‚Üíimport highspy
   165‚Üí
   166‚Üídef solve(input_data: dict) -> dict:
   167‚Üí    # Build and solve model
   168‚Üí    h = highspy.Highs()
   169‚Üí    h.silent()
   170‚Üí
   171‚Üí    # ... model building ...
   172‚Üí
   173‚Üí    h.run()
   174‚Üí
   175‚Üí    return {
   176‚Üí        'status': str(h.getModelStatus()),
   177‚Üí        'schedule': extract_schedule(h),
   178‚Üí        'objective': h.getInfo().objective_function_value,
   179‚Üí        'solveTime': h.getInfo().run_time
   180‚Üí    }
   181‚Üí
   182‚Üíif __name__ == '__main__':
   183‚Üí    input_data = json.load(sys.stdin)
   184‚Üí    result = solve(input_data)
   185‚Üí    print(json.dumps(result))
   186‚Üí```
   187‚Üí
   188‚Üí## Database: PostgreSQL
   189‚Üí
   190‚Üí### Why PostgreSQL
   191‚Üí
   192‚Üí```typescript
   193‚Üíinterface PostgreSQLBenefits {
   194‚Üí  reliability: 'Battle-tested, ACID compliant';
   195‚Üí  features: 'JSON, arrays, CTEs, window functions';
   196‚Üí  extensions: 'TimescaleDB, PostGIS, pg_cron';
   197‚Üí  performance: 'Excellent query optimizer';
   198‚Üí  operational: 'Well-understood operations';
   199‚Üí}
   200‚Üí```
   201‚Üí
   202‚Üí### Key Extensions
   203‚Üí
   204‚Üí```sql
   205‚Üí-- TimescaleDB for time-series metrics
   206‚ÜíCREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS timescaledb;
   207‚Üí
   208‚Üí-- Enable for mileage history table
   209‚ÜíSELECT create_hypertable('mileage_readings', 'recorded_at');
   210‚Üí
   211‚Üí-- pg_cron for scheduled jobs
   212‚ÜíCREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS pg_cron;
   213‚Üí
   214‚Üí-- Schedule daily RUC calculation
   215‚ÜíSELECT cron.schedule('daily-ruc', '0 5 * * *',
   216‚Üí  $$SELECT calculate_ruc()$$
   217‚Üí);
   218‚Üí```
   219‚Üí
   220‚Üí### Schema Example
   221‚Üí
   222‚Üí```typescript
   223‚Üí// drizzle/schema.ts
   224‚Üíimport { pgTable, uuid, text, integer, timestamp, real } from 'drizzle-orm/pg-core';
   225‚Üí
   226‚Üíexport const assets = pgTable('assets', {
   227‚Üí  id: uuid('id').primaryKey().defaultRandom(),
   228‚Üí  externalId: text('external_id').unique(),
   229‚Üí  name: text('name').notNull(),
   230‚Üí  assetType: text('asset_type').notNull(),
   231‚Üí  currentMileage: integer('current_mileage').default(0),
   232‚Üí  status: text('status').default('active'),
   233‚Üí  createdAt: timestamp('created_at').defaultNow(),
   234‚Üí  updatedAt: timestamp('updated_at').defaultNow()
   235‚Üí});
   236‚Üí
   237‚Üíexport const maintenanceTypes = pgTable('maintenance_types', {
   238‚Üí  id: uuid('id').primaryKey().defaultRandom(),
   239‚Üí  name: text('name').notNull(),
   240‚Üí  intervalMileage: integer('interval_mileage'),
   241‚Üí  intervalDays: integer('interval_days'),
   242‚Üí  durationHours: real('duration_hours').notNull(),
   243‚Üí  manHours: real('man_hours').notNull()
   244‚Üí});
   245‚Üí
   246‚Üíexport const scheduledEvents = pgTable('scheduled_events', {
   247‚Üí  id: uuid('id').primaryKey().defaultRandom(),
   248‚Üí  assetId: uuid('asset_id').references(() => assets.id),
   249‚Üí  maintenanceTypeId: uuid('maintenance_type_id').references(() => maintenanceTypes.id),
   250‚Üí  scheduledWeek: integer('scheduled_week').notNull(),
   251‚Üí  targetMileage: integer('target_mileage'),
   252‚Üí  status: text('status').default('scheduled'),
   253‚Üí  scheduleVersion: integer('schedule_version').notNull()
   254‚Üí});
   255‚Üí```
   256‚Üí
   257‚Üí## Cache: Redis
   258‚Üí
   259‚Üí### Why Redis
   260‚Üí
   261‚Üí```typescript
   262‚Üíinterface RedisBenefits {
   263‚Üí  speed: 'Microsecond latency';
   264‚Üí  versatile: 'Cache, queue, pub/sub, sessions';
   265‚Üí  simple: 'Easy to operate';
   266‚Üí  scalable: 'Cluster mode for HA';
   267‚Üí}
   268‚Üí```
   269‚Üí
   270‚Üí### Use Cases
   271‚Üí
   272‚Üí```typescript
   273‚Üí// Session caching
   274‚Üíawait redis.set(`session:${userId}`, JSON.stringify(session), 'EX', 3600);
   275‚Üí
   276‚Üí// API response caching
   277‚Üíconst cached = await redis.get(`schedule:${scheduleId}`);
   278‚Üíif (cached) return JSON.parse(cached);
   279‚Üí
   280‚Üí// Job queue
   281‚Üíawait redis.lpush('solver:jobs', JSON.stringify(job));
   282‚Üí
   283‚Üí// Pub/sub for real-time updates
   284‚Üíredis.publish('schedule:updated', JSON.stringify({ scheduleId }));
   285‚Üí```
   286‚Üí
   287‚Üí## Frontend: React + Next.js
   288‚Üí
   289‚Üí### Why React
   290‚Üí
   291‚Üí```typescript
   292‚Üíinterface ReactBenefits {
   293‚Üí  ecosystem: 'Largest component ecosystem';
   294‚Üí  hiring: 'Most developers know React';
   295‚Üí  tooling: 'Excellent development tools';
   296‚Üí  flexibility: 'Works with any backend';
   297‚Üí}
   298‚Üí```
   299‚Üí
   300‚Üí### Why Next.js
   301‚Üí
   302‚Üí```typescript
   303‚Üíinterface NextJSBenefits {
   304‚Üí  ssr: 'Server-side rendering for SEO and performance';
   305‚Üí  routing: 'File-based routing';
   306‚Üí  api: 'API routes for BFF pattern';
   307‚Üí  optimization: 'Image, font, script optimization';
   308‚Üí  deployment: 'Easy deployment to Vercel or self-host';
   309‚Üí}
   310‚Üí```
   311‚Üí
   312‚Üí### Project Structure
   313‚Üí
   314‚Üí```
   315‚Üíapps/web/
   316‚Üí‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ src/
   317‚Üí‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ app/                    # Next.js app router
   318‚Üí‚îÇ   ‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ layout.tsx
   319‚Üí‚îÇ   ‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ page.tsx            # Dashboard
   320‚Üí‚îÇ   ‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ schedule/
   321‚Üí‚îÇ   ‚îÇ   ‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ page.tsx        # Schedule view
   322‚Üí‚îÇ   ‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ assets/
   323‚Üí‚îÇ   ‚îÇ       ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ page.tsx        # Asset list
   324‚Üí‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ components/
   325‚Üí‚îÇ   ‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ ui/                 # Shadcn/ui components
   326‚Üí‚îÇ   ‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ schedule/           # Schedule-specific
   327‚Üí‚îÇ   ‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ charts/             # Visualization
   328‚Üí‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ lib/
   329‚Üí‚îÇ       ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ api.ts              # API client
   330‚Üí‚îÇ       ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ hooks/              # Custom hooks
   331‚Üí‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ next.config.js
   332‚Üí‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ package.json
   333‚Üí```
   334‚Üí
   335‚Üí## Visualization: ECharts
   336‚Üí
   337‚Üí### Why ECharts
   338‚Üí
   339‚Üí```typescript
   340‚Üíinterface EChartsBenefits {
   341‚Üí  performance: 'Handles 100k+ data points';
   342‚Üí  features: 'Gantt, heatmaps, calendars';
   343‚Üí  customization: 'Highly configurable';
   344‚Üí  opensource: 'Apache 2.0 license';
   345‚Üí}
   346‚Üí```
   347‚Üí
   348‚Üí### Schedule Visualization Example
   349‚Üí
   350‚Üí```typescript
   351‚Üíimport ReactECharts from 'echarts-for-react';
   352‚Üí
   353‚Üífunction ScheduleGantt({ events }: { events: ScheduledEvent[] }) {
   354‚Üí  const option = {
   355‚Üí    tooltip: { trigger: 'item' },
   356‚Üí    xAxis: {
   357‚Üí      type: 'time',
   358‚Üí      min: startDate,
   359‚Üí      max: endDate
   360‚Üí    },
   361‚Üí    yAxis: {
   362‚Üí      type: 'category',
   363‚Üí      data: assets.map(a => a.name)
   364‚Üí    },
   365‚Üí    series: [{
   366‚Üí      type: 'custom',
   367‚Üí      renderItem: renderGanttBar,
   368‚Üí      encode: { x: [0, 1], y: 2 },
   369‚Üí      data: events.map(e => [
   370‚Üí        e.startDate,
   371‚Üí        e.endDate,
   372‚Üí        e.assetIndex,
   373‚Üí        e.maintenanceType
   374‚Üí      ])
   375‚Üí    }]
   376‚Üí  };
   377‚Üí
   378‚Üí  return <ReactECharts option={option} style={{ height: 600 }} />;
   379‚Üí}
   380‚Üí```
   381‚Üí
   382‚Üí## Infrastructure
   383‚Üí
   384‚Üí### Docker Setup
   385‚Üí
   386‚Üí```dockerfile
   387‚Üí# Dockerfile for API
   388‚ÜíFROM oven/bun:1 AS base
   389‚ÜíWORKDIR /app
   390‚Üí
   391‚ÜíFROM base AS deps
   392‚ÜíCOPY package.json bun.lockb ./
   393‚ÜíRUN bun install --frozen-lockfile
   394‚Üí
   395‚ÜíFROM base AS runner
   396‚ÜíCOPY --from=deps /app/node_modules ./node_modules
   397‚ÜíCOPY . .
   398‚ÜíEXPOSE 3000
   399‚ÜíCMD ["bun", "run", "src/index.ts"]
   400‚Üí```
   401‚Üí
   402‚Üí```yaml
   403‚Üí# docker-compose.yml
   404‚Üíservices:
   405‚Üí  api:
   406‚Üí    build: ./apps/api
   407‚Üí    ports:
   408‚Üí      - "3000:3000"
   409‚Üí    environment:
   410‚Üí      - DATABASE_URL=postgresql://${DB_USER}:${DB_PASSWORD}@db:5432/mms
   411‚Üí      - REDIS_URL=redis://redis:6379

    26‚Üí1.0 Executive Summary 
    27‚ÜíZ
    28‚ÜíAZCART has thoroughly analyzed the Request for Quotation (RFQ) for 
    29‚Üíthe Mileage Management System. We understand the core business 
    30‚Üíobjective is to create a data-driven system that optimizes maintenance 
    31‚Üíscheduling to level-load resources, reduce costs, and ensure fleet availability. 
    32‚ÜíWe propose a strategic, phased partnership designed to deliver value at every 
    33‚Üístage while eliminating financial uncertainty and risk for both parties. 
    34‚ÜíOur engagement will begin with a Phase 0, where we will deliver a hands-on 
    35‚ÜíOptimization Playground, an interactive tool that will allow Keolis MHI 
    36‚Üístakeholders to simulate various operational scenarios and visualize the

1.0 Executive Summary 
Z
AZCART has thoroughly analyzed the Request for Quotation (RFQ) for 
the Mileage Management System. We understand the core business 
objective is to create a data-driven system that optimizes maintenance 
scheduling to level-load resources, reduce costs, and ensure fleet availability. 
We propose a strategic, phased partnership designed to deliver value at every 
stage while eliminating financial uncertainty and risk for both parties. 
Our engagement will begin with a Phase 0, where we will deliver a hands-on 
Optimization Playground, an interactive tool that will allow Keolis MHI 
stakeholders to simulate various operational scenarios and visualize the 
engine's value in real-time. This provides a dynamic and conclusive ROI 
assessment for the full system. 
For the subsequent development (Phases 1-3), we provide a guaranteed "Not to 
Exceed" (NTE) budget. This is the maximum possible cost. Upon completion of 
Phase 0, we will convert this NTE into a lower, final fixed-price quote, passing 
the savings from the reduced risk directly on to Keolis MHI. 
This SOW details the scope, deliverables, and commercial terms for this 
transparent, collaborative, and value-driven partnership. 


## Page 2

2.0 Our Understanding of the Challenge 
Based on the RFQ and our analysis, the primary challenges are: 
‚Ä¢
Resource Bottlenecks: Un-optimized maintenance schedules lead to 
peaks and troughs in the demand for skilled labor and maintenance 
tracks, increasing costs and operational risk. 
‚Ä¢
Lack of Predictive Insight: A manual, reactive process makes it difficult 
to forecast future needs and make proactive, data-driven decisions for 
train and run assignments. 
‚Ä¢
Data Fragmentation: Mileage and maintenance data are stored in 
disparate systems, preventing a single, unified view of fleet health and 
operational readiness. 
3.0 The Proposed Solution: A Phased Partnership 
We propose a gated, multiphase project. This structure ensures that each phase 
is built upon a solid, data-validated foundation. 
Phase 0: Discovery & Optimization Playground (3 Weeks) 
‚Ä¢
A collaborative deep-dive into your data models and business rules. 
‚Ä¢
Deliverable: An exclusive, access-controlled Optimization 
Playground. This secure web-based simulator allows your team to: 
‚Ä¢
Generate and test various operational scenarios. 
‚Ä¢
Instantly visualize the comparison between un-optimized and optimized 
maintenance plans. 
‚Ä¢
Clearly see the value of Resource Leveling, as the engine transforms 
volatile workload peaks and troughs into a smooth, predictable demand 
on workshop resources. 
Phase 1: MVP Software Implementation 
‚Ä¢
Development of the core Progressive Web App (PWA) with dashboards 
for planners and operators. 
‚Ä¢
Implementation of the daily tactical recommendation engines (RUC and 
RunAssignment). 
Phase 2: Costing Module & Advanced Reporting 
‚Ä¢
Integration of financial data to enable total cost of ownership (TCO) 
analysis. 
‚Ä¢
Development of a comprehensive, multi-format reporting module. 
Phase 3: Full Integration & Enterprise Readiness 
‚Ä¢
Implementation of the IBM Maximo integration adapter. 
‚Ä¢
Deployment of the automated AlertEngine with email notifications. 


## Page 3

4.0 Commercial Framework 
Our commercial model is designed for transparency and shared success. 
4.1. Phase 0: Discovery & Optimization Blueprint 
‚Ä¢
Structure: Fixed Price 
‚Ä¢
Scope: As detailed in Appendix A. 
‚Ä¢
Total Fixed Price: $12,500.00 USD 
‚Ä¢
Payment Terms: Due within 15 days following the final presentation and 
the conclusion of the 3-week access period to the Optimization 
Playground. 
4.2. Phases 1-3: Full System Implementation 
‚Ä¢
Structure: Not to Exceed (NTE) budget, executed on a Time & Materials 
basis, with a final Fixed-Price conversion. 
‚Ä¢
Process: 
1.
The NTE amounts below represent the guaranteed maximum cost for 
each phase. 
2.
Work will be billed against the NTE based on actual hours worked at the 
agreed-upon rate. 
3.
Upon completion of Phase 0, ZAZCART will present a final, lower fixed-
price quote for each phase. Keolis MHI will benefit from the cost savings 
realized through the de-risking process. 
4.3. Project Continuance 
Upon completion of Phase 0, Keolis MHI has the option, but not the obligation, 
to proceed with the subsequent phases by issuing a "Notice to Proceed". This 
structure provides a formal, no-risk off-ramp while maintaining a clear path for 
the full project. 
5.0 About ZAZCART 
Established in 2016, ZAZCART is a specialized engineering firm dedicated to 
the 
architecture and deployment of mission-critical software systems for complex 
operational environments. We provide high-assurance engineering solutions

Every system component is engineered for resilience, auditability and

Appendix A: Detailed SOW for Phase 0 
A.1 Scope: The scope is a 3-week engagement to develop and provide access 
to a secure, interactive Optimization Playground. This includes: 
1.
Development of a stateless web application hosted on ZAZCART's 
secure infrastructure. The application is designed to process data in-

operational scenarios. This eliminates the need to use sensitive client

allowing for hands-on "what-if" analysis. 
2.
Real-time execution of the ZAZCART optimization engine on the 
generated synthetic data. 
3.
A clear, graphical side-by-side comparison of "before" and "after" 
optimization results, demonstrating the tangible value in resource 
leveling and bottleneck reduction. 
4.
A final presentation summarizing the findings from the simulations and 
presenting a refined, data-validated scope for Phases 1-3.

input.prompt: List ALL React UI component libraries mentioned, especially focusing on: 1) Enterprise/data-heavy libraries, 2) Libraries with strong table/grid components, 3) Libraries with chart integration, 4) Libraries designed for dashboards. # React UI Component Libraries - Enterprise & Data-Heavy Focus

## Enterprise/Data-Heavy Libraries

**Blueprint** - "A React-based UI toolkit for building complex, data-dense web interfaces for desktop applications." Designed specifically for intricate enterprise applications requiring sophisticated component hierarchies.

**Fabric** - Microsoft's official React implementation offering "a set of React components for building experiences for Office and Office 365," with extensive enterprise support and TypeScript implementation.

**Elastic UI (EUI)** - "The Elastic UI Framework is a collection of React UI components for quickly building user interfaces at Elastic." Optimized for information-rich dashboards and monitoring tools.

**PatternFly React** - Built on Bootstrap 3, offering "a set of React components for the PatternFly project," specifically engineered for enterprise product interfaces.

**Ant Design** - "An enterprise-class UI design language and React-based implementation." Includes specialized pro version for complex business applications.

## Libraries with Strong Table/Grid Components

**Material-UI** - Industry standard with extensive table implementations; "Components that implement Google's Material Design."

**Prime React** - Explicitly offers "over 60 UI components with multiple themes," emphasizing data table capabilities for business applications.

**RSuite** - Described as "a set of react component libraries for enterprise system products," particularly strong in data presentation.

**Base UI (Uber)** - "A React Component library implementing the Base UI design" with focus on scalable data interfaces.

## Dashboard-Oriented Libraries

**Grommet** - "React-based framework that provides accessibility, modularity, responsiveness, and theming," ideal for responsive dashboard layouts.

**Carbon (IBM)** - "React components according to IBM's Carbon design system," extensively used in monitoring and analytics platforms.

**Instructure UI** - "A UI Component Library made by Instructure, Inc" supporting complex educational dashboard interfaces.

---

**Note:** Most libraries listed lack explicit chart integration mentions in this source material.
